description: This figure presents SEM tensile fracture morphologies of hot-rolled Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels with different Be contents: (a) x=0 (0Be steel), (b) x=0.5 wt% (0.5Be steel), (c) x=1.0 wt% (1.0Be steel), (d) x=1.5 wt% (1.5Be steel). The inset in each subgraph shows the macroscopic fracture morphology, with the marked area corresponding to the magnified SEM view. 0Be and 0.5Be steels exhibit typical ductile fracture features dominated by dimples, with few cleavage planes observed. As Be content increases to 1.0 wt% and 1.5 wt%, the area of cleavage surfaces at the fracture increases gradually, and obvious cleavage steps are visible in the 1.5Be sample, indicating the fracture mode transforms from fully ductile fracture to ductile-brittle mixed fracture with increasing Be addition. This observation correlates with the decreased elongation of 1.0Be and 1.5Be steels, and supports the conclusion that excessive Be addition introduces brittle Be-rich precipitates and degrades tensile ductility of the test steels.
